questions.prompt: Which valuation method discounts the unlevered free cash flow at the unlevered cost of capital, then adds the PV of the tax shield separately?
questions.explanation: APV explicitly separates operating value from the value of financing-related tax shields. Useful when the debt schedule is known.

questions.prompt: WACC is the most natural choice when:
choices: The firm maintains a stable D/V ratio over time
Debt is scheduled to pay down rapidly
You want equity value directly without separately computing debt
questions.explanation: WACC implicitly assumes constant D/V. For LBOs or project finance with declining leverage, APV is more accurate.

questions.prompt: If WACC, APV, and FTE give different valuations for the same project, the most likely causes are:
choices: Cost of equity not properly levered for the assumed D/E
Capital structure changes over time being ignored in WACC
The three methods are fundamentally different and can't agree
questions.explanation: In a consistent setup, all three methods give the same answer. Disagreement = inconsistent inputs, mixed CF definitions, or treating WACC as constant when capital structure varies.

questions.prompt: Perpetual after-tax FCF of $10M, $40M of perpetual debt at 5%, tax rate 25%, unlevered cost of capital r_U = 11%. Compute total firm value V via APV. Answer in $M.

furtherReading.title: A Comparison of the APV, FTE, and WACC Approaches
furtherReading.why: The textbook section that lays out when WACC, APV, and FTE each give the right answer — directly mirrors the quiz's three-method framing.
